speaker: interviewer
text: Six. Tape number six, interview with Selma Engel. You are in this small village with a baby. And you were starting to tell us what Chaim was doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, Chaim went to different places. And [INAUDIBLE]-- oh, he suffered, too. Sometimes once the police came and they saw what he had-- it was not allowed. I don't know why it was not allowed. And they took all his things. And he'll tell the story. Oh, he will not tell the story. But, anyway, we-- and I was all alone. And we were so poor. We didn't have one pail. What I cooked in, was a hole and I filled it up with bread. And when it cooked sometimes it fell out. And we cooked on a stove. And the stove didn't give heat enough, and then we got something else. And then we made it with the straw-- from wood. I call it, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Shavings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the shaving from wood. And we put it on and the hole and blows up. And whole wall was-- house was-- it was horrible. And at night, where we stepped, the ice when we forgot to formed ice. And from-- the windowsill was no-- was very cold there in Pole. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you speak any Pol--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't speak a word Polish. So finally I could say milk, and could say breads, and vegetables. And was no water in that house. We had to go up the middle in that little village. You had to walk there. And there was a pump and they get water out. And when there was ice, you had to walk. It was so far. I couldn't speak Polish. I couldn't speak Yiddish with the Jews who were left over. Only thing-- what I could do was cry the whole day. I cried the whole day. I couldn't speak with the baby. [INAUDIBLE] speak with the baby. I cried the whole day. The neighbors made the same noise already that I did. I was so unhappy. I thought I could have better sit in jail than-- I was very unhappy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, finally, we found out that this girl-- Oolah Stern-- lived in Wlodawa, not far where we are. And she was also very unhappy, and she had a very, very hard time. So we brought her over to us. And Chaim built another bed. But he couldn't do anything, but he build another bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she slept-- it was a two rooms. And she slept in the other room, and we slept in that room. So thank God I had somebody. And that was wonderful because she-- we got very good friends. And we were friends. And she-- we didn't have anything. And she had no clothes, so finely Chaim bought some things. Underpants for us so we had to get clean underpants. And she had a coat, so I could wear her coat. And she had boots, and I didn't have boots. And I had old shoes-- yeah. I took the boots was from that we run away. So we shared together, and we had-- we could talk together. And I remember the soldiers, they came-- the Russian soldiers came through the street. And they sang so nice, so she want to go outside. So she polished her shoes specially very nice, and I had to see how nice she looked. So, you know, we had a little bit fun together. And once in awhile Chaim came home and brought some food, money, and some food. And then we could go out and buy some food. So, and the war was still going on, because the Germans were standing a half year before Warsaw. And Warsaw was more-- we were on the side from the-- on the other side from Warsaw, more to Russia, by the brook.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were on the Eastern side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were on the Eastern side, so we were already a half year before Russia was freed. We were free. [? It's ?] [? slow. ?] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So, and I-- what could I do? The only thing what I could do was taking care of the baby. Chaim kept [INAUDIBLE] some Russian Jews. He spoke Russian in that time. He forgot it now. But he spoke Russian, so he could speak with the Russian Jews. And he came sometimes over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we had already a little bit more Jews over so could understand us. They spoke German. The Russian Jews spoke Yiddish with Chaim when Chaim was there. And Oolah was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had-- I was already a little bit more-- we were very poor. We had really nothing. And Chaim brought sometimes a chicken home, or a turkey home. And once he brought a turkey home, and I didn't know how to cook a turkey. I didn't know how to cook. And I did know a little bit to cook, but Dutch. But in Holland. Not like giving a turkey with feathers and everything. What do I do? I had no idea. So I put it outside, and once a Russian Jew come-- a Russian officer will come and eat that. So I put him so long outside-- a turkey-- out the porch. And instead froze. Froze. Oh God. And then I had to clean it. And oh, was-- you know was-- that was-- a moment was a difficult time. For us, too, another difficult time.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have to be worried about-- there was a Russian [? public ?] [? room ?] but state. And the Russian soldiers went to them. And once a Russian came upstairs. Chaim wasn't there so I hold a baby in front of me. And they went-- thank God, they went out again. But, you know, we still know we were free. And how much-- oh, I was upset. And I was a spoiled brat-- only girl from four children, and the youngest. But, you know, we made it, especially with Oolah was there. And then also one day-- and Oolah went down already to Lublin. We were not far from Lublin. And she went to Lublin. There was also a-- later on met some more Jewish people there and some survivors from Sobibor. And there was one woman survivor what was like a mother from everybody. And Oolah went to her. In the end, she went to her. And one day the [INAUDIBLE]-- one of the Polish Jews say to Chaim, you know, we should go away. The Partisan are planning to kill the few Jews that are left in parts of it are leftover. So middle of the night we had a truck-- somebody-- and one of the Jews had a truck, I think. And we took a little bit things with us, what we had, and we went to Lublin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, once was a fire. I forgot to tell. Once was a fire. Chaim told me about this one fire. The whole village is [INAUDIBLE]. All very old, wooden houses. So, I was very upset. And Oolah, and everything, what we had, the little bit, brought outside. But nothing was happened. And the way-- there was no fire hose or so. It was little cans. They made the fire out, you know? So, we went to Lublin. And it was one Polish Jew that first run away from Sobibor at least about perhaps few months before. And he-- Portlevnik was his name. And he made it. Now, after that 13 Jews were killed because he run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he and a friend run away. And they made it. They made it to America. And they died very young. Both of them died very young. Not-- we never met them, I think, till we came here to America. And I was 35 when I came to America. And we went to his house. Chaim asked if we could stay with him. And he say, yes. A lot of Jews lived there already. It was very-- he made a lot of money. He handled with the Russian. He bought loads of-- truck loads of sugar, and salt, and something like that. He made big, big business. So we stayed there together, in that house. Now, Chaim went away when somebody owned him some money. And he went-- one night he went away. And that night somebody-- he went a few nights away. Some man came and-- Polish Jew and he hit on the door and said, Portlevnik! Portlevnik! And he knows that it was not kosher. There was somebody wanted to kill--

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody was looking--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Poles want to kill the Jews. And he went to our door and they put the lights out. And everybody put the money-- we had on the baby already. Put the money under the baby's mattress. And we didn't say anything. And somehow they went away. And for the few people, the very few mostly went away. But they killed a few-- they killed some Jews in the same building where we were at. And they didn't kill us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to stay in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. No! Pole. No. No. We want to go back to Holland. I want to go Holland. I tell Chaim how Holland-- how beautiful it is, and the houses are so beautiful, and the people are so nice. And what's not-- but there were-- you know, I've-- I told him about the street. I tell him all the cobble stones in the street. And I tell him about Holland, how beautiful Holland was. And but I was a little girl where I grow up. And he-- we want to go to Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, one day [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he met a girl-- an officer. Chaim met an officer, a Dutch officer. And somehow he felt very Dutch. He spoke, perhaps, a little bit Dutch, and he has language-- he speaks-- he was three months in Holland and he spoke Dutch. And he was three months when we left -- two months in Holland [INAUDIBLE] my head-- easily. He-- Jewish background, he spoke Hebrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, he spoke with Dutch man and he say, we're planning to get a whole bunch of Dutch people together and go over Romania, Hungary, to Russia and go so over the [INAUDIBLE] to Marseilles. So, and that one day came. And we went with a whole bunch of Dutch people-- not all Jews. We're also German-- Dutch people would say they were German. And perhaps they were, perhaps, Nazis, who knows. And we went in a big, big train-- long train. Whole bunch of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was financing all this? Who was paying for all this? Did you have to pay your own way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't think-- that was just organized from the Red Cross, I think. The Red Cross was all give us food, too, in the train. I think the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the first time that we hear something from the Red Cross. And that was the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And so you were taking-- going by train through Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, freight-- with a freight train. And were 80, 90 people. And they made-- the train, it had a two upstairs and downstairs. We slept down with the baby. And Oolah was in another train together. Yeah, Oolah was in-- was Oolah in the train? Yeah, Oolah was in the train, too. Yeah, Oolah was in train, too. And we were then-- and on the moment too Oolah had found all young people. I belonged already to the old generation. She belonged-- she was younger than I am. She was four or five years younger than I am, so she had a lot of young friends already. But was, oh, also together in that train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we drove-- we went-- that took-- I think it took months that we went in this train. And the train stopped, and it went farther. And we didn't-- we had something to eat. They give us something to eat. I think from of the Red Cross, was that. And they give us something to eat. And when the train stopped we run out. And we had some dishes to wash. And we had to get some hot water from the-- how you call it? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] The engine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from the engine. We took some water-- hot water from the engine, and washed out the diapers with. And then we jumped in. And once we were upstair and the train started rolling, and we run quickly. And we lost our place, what we had. And quickly we jumped on the train and the baby was in the train. So that took a long, long time--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1944. 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: The war was-- the war was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The war was still going on in Europe. If not, we would have gone this way over Germany, you know? But we couldn't go over Germany. It was still war. So we went the other way. And but we don't know, perhaps who knew how long that war will hold. You had no idea. It was stupid, because when there's war there, there would be war there, too. My-- no, but then we went to Russia. Excuse me. Then we were in Czernowitz And it was in a camp in Czernowitz. And that was nice, a very little camp. I had dysentery. And also the baby had dysentery, because I nursed the baby and I had dysentery. And so this very contagious, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were in a camp and there were Gypsies there. But they killed the Gypsies, too, in the war. Not only the Jews. They killed the Gypsies, too. So there were a lot of Gypsies left over. And they were there and played music. Not that I went there, because I was busy with this baby. The baby was sick. And we tried-- we had no money. Did we have money? We had some money. We were tough luck. We were already-- the people would wear costumes from-- would wear in the group and the from the concentration camp, these striped clothes. And there were Jews in Czernowitz which were free and they got everything back, because I don't think they-- they didn't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do anything to the Jews till the last year, or half year, in Romania. Something like that. So some had the business. And they all had the uniform. They all got food delivered to the Jewish families there. And we didn't because we were already in normal clothes. So we very jealous on all these people. Once we need medicine, and that's-- drugstore did the uppertaker give us free. So, anyway, we were there a few months. And then I had to go-- the baby was so sick I had to go in the hospital. I went to a Russian hospital. I lay with the baby in a Russian hospital. I remember there was some kids who had syphilis. And I was so afraid that-- I had no idea how you get syphilis. That how-- our baby will get also syphilis. So, anyway, we stayed there in that hospital and there was nothing there. There was no bed. There was a little bed, but there was no blankets or anything. And the Russian doctors didn't know what to do with us. So, finally, Chaim-- the train stopped far ago. In middle of the night, Chaim comes to go to the hospital and write me out, and we take the risk all  by ourself. And the train went farther and went farther and we ended up in Odessa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went in a camp where all the people went for what Russian people go for vacations, to the Black Sea. And there we stayed in a camp, in zone camp. And that was the Red Cross was already had waking up completely, also I think with the train, and they took care of us. And from that time on we had good food and baby healed completely, and I healed completely. And we got some clothes. We didn't get much clothes. Somehow we bought-- I don't know. We went-- I was all-- Chaim would say, Selma, Selma, forget it. Forget it. Let them get it. Let them-- you know, he's always the one, stay calm. Don't do anything. That's Chaim. Very nice man. He always says, Selma.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't get clothes for the baby. I did once [INAUDIBLE]. We didn't-- we get nothing. But, anyway, we had-- we got food from the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Chaim could go out a little bit by himself. I was stuck with this baby and never saw anything for Odessa. But it was wonderful. It was nice weather. It was on the Black Sea. We were sitting on the sea, but I also don't remember much. But it was OK. And one day was a big boat. And I forgot the name, too. The same boat where the father from Anne Frank went back to Holland. I was in the same-- I met the father from Anne Frank in Odessa.

speaker: interviewer
text: You met Otto Frank in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Otto Frank, I met in Odessa. He was much older than I am. 20 years older. I didn't-- I didn't have much to do with him. I had just a baby. And Chaim and I, we just lived for the baby and me. And Chaim-- was a Dutch captain-- what I told you was how I met-- and he organized it. There's always one big shot. No, one organizer what holds the ball together. And he say to Chaim, don't talk. And don't say anything. So Chaim didn't talk. And he was done go out that he's Dutch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Chaim could not have gotten on the boat--

speaker: interviewee
text: On a boat because he was Polish. So when we went on the boat, smuggled on the boat, I was a nervous wreck and I didn't have milk for the baby. And I went to the kitchen, and the baby was in between nine months old and it was very healthy. There was nothing wrong with him. Anyway, perhaps he had dysentery, who knows. Anyway, we never saw a doctor. All these years not. I didn't see a doctor. When I was pregnant I didn't see a doctor. Once a doctor saw when the baby was very sick. [DRINKS] We were on the boat and everything was fine. The baby got sick and 24 hours later it dies. The boat was in the waters already on the [INAUDIBLE] Aegesa Sea.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Aegean Sea.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the baby was buried in the sea. And 80 days later we were in Holland. We went to Marseilles. And the people were fantastic in Marseilles to us. They were so very wonderful. They give us to eat. And they want to stay-- we didn't want to stay in people's houses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went over Belgium. And the people give us there to eat. And then we came in Holland, and they say, more people? We don't have to eat for ourself. What are you doing here? And we stayed in quarantine. Quarantine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quarantine because it's the Dutch people. And then they brought us in a freight car, on top of freight car, to my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in May. We were in May in Odessa and we heard the war was over. Somebody had a radio so you could follow that the war was over. So that was in-- March, May-- April, perhaps. At the end of May. At the end of May. Perhaps. The end of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you got to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Got to Holland. And so and then we-- brought us in the freight car to my hometown. I went to a town where my uncle and aunt lived. And I don't know if they say to somebody, tell Dr. Horen that Selma Engel is alive. So they told him, and they right away came in contact with us. And it was wonderful. Because that was like-- that later on like our father and mother. And then we came to a hotel, to Zwolle. And we-- they-- and then I walked through the hotel and I started whistling. We have a whistle. And my brother was sick--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does the whistle sound like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that whistle. I remember the whistle. We had a new whistle, Chaim and I. Chaim came home every time when he came from working, he whistled-- [WHISTLES] And so that's the whistle from our children and us when my son had used a whistle and [INAUDIBLE] used to whistle. And so every time then I would know it's Chaim. Middle of the night I go up and I know Chaim is there. So and then I whistle the whistle from-- and he came down. And he thought he was sick that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who? This is your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother. And he thought he's not sick. He thought he's crazy, too. And he for sure thought that I was dead. And perhaps he wasn't-- no. Anyway, and he had three children in the war. And he-- and his wife was pregnant from the fourth one. And it was a blessing. This gets our best friends and our-- like our own children. And then we were in Zwolle and they stayed in the hotel. We came to the hotel. And the hotel--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your brother had gotten the hotel back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the hotel was there. So he went and moved into the hotel. He didn't get evicted. He moved in the hotel. And the way it was, he didn't clean it. He didn't do anything. And my sister-in-law had-- never did anything in her whole life. Still doesn't. But now she's sick and almost dying.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we came there, we clean it up, Chaim and I. And we made little rooms upstairs from the stage where, you know, with the columns in front of the stage. There was a stage in the hotel on the ballroom where the people could stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there were a lot of Canadian soldiers. They had nowhere to sleep, so they sleep in these little rooms. And then they paid us for that. And so we slowly-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] life started to be a home. When I came there they had a child exactly our child's age. My brother and [INAUDIBLE]. And we-- and then we found out what everything what happened. That the whole family was killed. That our mother didn't come back, and my brothers, and my sister-in-law. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And the German-- the Dutch people were not nice to us. And they say, you are not married. And then the family doctor, which was a very nice man, he saw me with Chaim in the city. And I used had still milk in my breasts. And he say, oh, you come also with something like that? Somebody had-- a girl came with guy from Germany, from the German camp or so.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, they didn't believe. I went away as an 18 years old girl, and I come back with an-- and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was not-- you didn't come home. That's the world. You didn't came home. You came to a country where the people didn't like the Jews at all. But I didn't know. There was-- there were some Jews. But some people were liked. But they didn't help us with anything. And Holland was nothing. It was an unfriendly country when we came back from Holland. Not the country what I remember when I grow up in. And so I start also looking for my family. I went to-- we had a large family, what I told you. And we had-- my mother had a lot of cousins, what was not from brothers and sisters. And we were very close with all of them. And I went to the cities where they came from, and nothing was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, and then we didn't get along. I didn't get along with my sister-in-law. The business did well. We made money. We made good money. We didn't get along. We lived on top of each other. There was-- they call it hotel. It was just ten little rooms on the top. And we were next to each other. It's still there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have a little room. And she had a little room. And Chaim and I were really were the on that work there. But that was not a bad thing. It was just-- we had done in between, we got-- later on we have two grand-- of course, later on. A few years we had two children of our own. But it didn't work out. It was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived-- six years we lived in Holland. And then also Polish Jew, Chaim didn't have-- in Holland you have to have-- Chaim was a businessman. In Holland you have to have-- everything you have-- you have to go to school at least three years to get an-- [DUTCH]. Something where you open something?

speaker: interviewer
text: A license?

speaker: interviewee
text: License or something to go. And you couldn't get a license. Oh, we got a license from one family what died in concentration camp. And we got the license to open a textile store. And Holland was not built in all these years, so you couldn't get a store. So there was a family what had a store, and they didn't have a license to open it. And I didn't want to start a business with them, but Chaim was desperate to get out of that hotel. We were both desperate to get out of the hotel, but did very well, and we made very money. When it was a kettle market. And was every Friday we made a lot of money. But we didn't get along, so what has-- all the money has nothing to do with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we get the license. That was also in Holland. You have to have a license. And the license was of the name for my father and mother, and you could write it over once. And Chaim say, your brother is the oldest from the family, and he's the only one that came back. He deserves it. So he gave the license to my brother for that hotel. And my sister-in-law was not a house wife, so the hotel went down, down, down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the people where we want to go to business with were not very nice people. But Chaim was so desperate, so we started a business with these people, textile store. And we did very well. We bought-- we built a house. Also from the money-- we got a little bit money from the German-- from the Dutch government, because what we went through. And they stole-- the Germans stole. So we bought-- we build a house. A little house. And we did well, and everything was fine. And then the people that we took the business with-- and then came the Korean War. And the business went debt. I think over the whole world was-- we didn't do well. And then the people what we had the business with, they swindled Chaim for everything. And so that was in 1950. That was 1950.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had then already two kids. We had a beautiful house with everything we could want. What do you have? Insurance. We had insurance. And when we had another-- he didn't make a living. We couldn't-- and he couldn't start a business. So he started a business from ice cream in another town. So can you imagine Chaim cannot cook water. So and it was also that year was a rain and very cold. So people didn't buy ice cream. So we lost everything. And I always wanted to go to Israel. Did Chaim want to leave Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I mean, no. He had no choice. Because he had to go three years to school. And how could he go to three years of school? We had already two children. What will I do to make a living? I didn't have-- we didn't have any money in between. No, we had some money, yeah. We had some money. But not enough to make a living. And I-- what could I do to make a living for a family with two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't want to stay in Holland. I-- what did I have in Holland? There was nobody of the family there. We have nobody. Only my  brother and the four children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister-in-law was not nice. She didn't want that I speak to the children. The children were not allowed to come to our house. We had no friendship at all. And a few Jews were left there, we didn't have any contact with them. No-- with them at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wanted to go to Israel and he wanted to go to America. You couldn't come in to America, Polish quota. So what-- and it was very easy to go to Israel. So I had this uncle and aunt from Apeldoorn, the doctor what I told you. He was in Israel. And he went with all-- with his children, with his three children. They went to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were very well-to-do. And they lived up the Caramel. And they say, come to us. No, they didn't say that. But they say, when you come on the [INAUDIBLE] come to us. We went-- we had a connection with a kibbutz in Israel, Moledet. And it was a kibbutz from [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
